⚗️ Île de Crespo
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

1. Équipements : En 1866, les scaphandres étaient rudimentaires, suspendus à la paroi du sous-marin Nautilus pour permettre aux membres d'équipage de sortir et observer le milieu marin. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les scuba (scaphandre autonome sous-marin) sont plus avancés avec des respirateurs à oxygène comprimé et des masques à visière pour une meilleure visibilité.

2. Exploration : En 1866, la première chasse sous-marine a lieu sur l'île de Crespo, où les membres d'équipage ont découvert une forêt sous-marine et une monstrueuse araignée de mer. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les explorations sous-marines sont plus avancées grâce à des robots sous-marins équipés de caméras haute définition pour filmer et étudier les écosystèmes marins.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

3. Espèces : En 1866, les espèces observées sur l'île de Crespo comprenaient des baleines, narvals, cétacés, mollusques comme le moule et des crustacés comme l'homard. Aujourd'hui en 2025, de nombreuses espèces marines ont été découvertes, notamment la grande raie à piquant bleu (Manta birostris) et la baleine bleue (Balaenoptera musculus), ainsi que des espèces rares comme le corail de mer rose (Corallium rubrum).

4. Technologies : En 1866, les technologies sous-marines étaient très limitées, avec le Nautilus étant l'une des premières sous-marins à propulsion électrique. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les technologies sous-marines ont grandement évolué, avec des sous-marins capables de plonger jusqu'à 11 000 mètres et équipés de systèmes d'observation avancés pour étudier le climat océanique.

⚗️ Crespo Island
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Nautilus stopped at Crespo Island for an underwater hunt, where various marine species were observed such as whales, narwhals, cetaceans, mussels, lobsters, and crabs. The submarine forest was a fascinating sight, yet little was known about its biodiversity or ecological significance. In contrast, in 2025, advanced submersibles equipped with AI-powered biodiversity sensors would explore the same underwater landscape, providing detailed insights into the complex ecosystem of Crespo Island's seafloor.

In 1866, the sighting of a monstrous sea spider was an unusual occurrence, and its scientific classification remained uncertain due to limited knowledge about deep-sea fauna. By 2025, advances in marine biology had led to the discovery and identification of numerous new species, including giant isopods and yeti crabs, providing a better understanding of the bizarre creatures that inhabit the depths of the ocean.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, Professor Aronnax's equipment was limited to traditional scientific instruments like microscopes and compasses. By 2025, researchers would utilize cutting-edge technology such as miniature robots, genetic sequencing devices, and underwater drones, enabling them to gather vast amounts of data about the marine environment with unprecedented precision and speed.

In 1866, Captain Nemo's hunting party aimed to demonstrate the Nautilus' technological superiority by capturing various marine species for observation. In 2025, conservation efforts prioritize non-invasive research methods to minimize disturbance to fragile ecosystems. Advanced submersibles and remote sensing technologies allow scientists to study marine life without causing harm or altering natural habitats.

⚗️ Archipel des Pomotou
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

Archipel des Pomotou : superficie

En 1866, l'archipel couvrait une superficie de trois cent soixante-dix lieues carrées. Aujourd'hui, en 2025, les technologies de cartographie et d'imagerie satellitaire permettent de mesurer des surfaces plus précises. L'espace occupé par l'archipel des Pomotou est estimé à environ 640 km², soit une réduction de la moitié de sa superficie initiale.

Archipel des Pomotou : espèces marines observées

En 1866, les espèces observées dans l'archipel incluaient des cétacés, requins, raies, thons, harengs, lampreys, gobiés, calmar et autres. Aujourd'hui, la biodiversité marine est mieux connue grâce aux études scientifiques. Les espèces observées dans l'archipel comprennent des cétacés comme le baleine bleue ou le dauphin commun, des requins tels que le requin-tigre et le requin-baleine, ainsi que des raies manta.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

Archipel des Pomotou : technologie marine

En 1866, le Nautilus de Jules Verne explorait l'archipel avec une technologie fictive. Aujourd'hui, en 2025, les navires utilisent des technologies modernes pour explorer les mers profondes. Les sous-marins d'exploration sont équipés de sonars, caméras et autres instruments de mesure pour cartographier les fonds marins et observer la vie marine.

Archipel des Pomotou : pollution marine

En 1866, dans le roman de Jules Verne, l'archipel était pollué par des coques naufragées et des objets de fer dégradés. Aujourd'hui, en 2025, la pollution marine est un problème majeur pour les écosystèmes marins. Les déchets plastiques, les pollutions chimiques et les effets du réchauffement climatique sont des sources de pollution marine qui ont été identifiées par les chercheurs. Les initiatives de nettoyage et de prévention de la pollution marine sont en cours pour protéger l'archipel des Pomotou et d'autres écosystèmes marins fragiles.

⚗️ Pomotou Archipelago
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Pomotou Archipelago was largely unexplored, covering an area of approximately 370 square leagues with about sixty island groups. The marine life observed included various species such as cetaceans, sharks, rays, tuna, herring, lamprey, goby, and squid. Shipwrecks were common, with remnants like the Florida still visible in the depths.

In contrast, by 2025, extensive research and exploration have been conducted on the Pomotou Archipelago. Marine life surveys have revealed a more diverse ecosystem than previously known, including new species such as the deep-sea coral Lophelia pertusa and the elusive giant squid Architeuthis dux.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, shipwrecks were a common sight due to the lack of advanced navigation and communication technologies. However, by 2025, satellite technology and global positioning systems (GPS) have significantly reduced maritime accidents, making such sights rare. Instead, underwater archaeologists often study historical wrecks for insights into past civilizations.

In terms of marine conservation, the situation in 1866 was largely unregulated. By 2025, however, international agreements like the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) and regional fisheries management organizations have been established to protect marine ecosystems from overfishing and pollution. The Pomotou Archipelago is now part of a network of protected areas, ensuring the preservation of its unique biodiversity for future generations.

⚗️ Archipel des nouvelles Hébrides
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

1. Découverte : En 1866, l'archipel des Nouvelles Hébrides a été découvert par Quiros en 1606, exploré par Bougainville en 1768 et reçu son nom actuel par Cook en 1773. En 2025, cet archipel est toujours connu sous le même nom et continue de fasciner les explorateurs et les touristes.

2. Espèces marines : En 1866, des espèces observées sur place comprenaient le cétacé, le thon, le saumon, la dorade, l'anguille, l'huître, l'oursin et le corail. En 2025, ces mêmes espèces sont encore présentes dans les eaux de l'archipel, mais des études de biologie marine ont également révélé la présence d'autres espèces marines telles que le requin tigre et la tortue verte.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

3. Technologie : En 1866, le Nautilus navigait dans les eaux de l'archipel à l'aide des voiles et de la force humaine. En 2025, les navires modernes utilisent une technologie plus avancée, tels que les moteurs diesel et électriques, pour naviguer dans les eaux de l'archipel.

4. Nourriture : En 1866, le Nautilus se nourrissait en consommant des douzaines d'huîtres pour satisfaire la nourriture quotidienne d'un homme. En 2025, les humains ont développé de nouvelles techniques de pêche et de production animale pour satisfaire leurs besoins alimentaires, ce qui permet aux voyageurs modernes de profiter de plus variées cuisines lorsqu'ils visitent l'archipel des Nouvelles Hébrides.

⚗️ New Hebrides Archipelago
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the New Hebrides Archipelago was a largely unexplored region, with its discovery by Quiros in 1606 and exploration by Bougainville in 1768 still relatively recent events. Cook named it in 1773. Marine life observed included cetaceans, tuna, salmon, sea bream, sparidae, oysters, sea urchins, and coral, all of which were studied primarily through visual observation.

In contrast, by 2025, the New Hebrides Archipelago has been extensively researched, with advancements in technology allowing for more detailed studies of marine life. The same species observed in 1866 can still be found, but modern tools such as sonar and remote-operated submersibles have enabled scientists to study these organisms in greater depth. For example, genetic sequencing has been used to understand the evolutionary history of coral reefs, while satellite imagery has helped monitor changes in ocean currents and temperature affecting marine life.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, the marine ecosystem was relatively untouched by human activity, with fishing primarily done through traditional methods. By 2025, overfishing and pollution have become significant concerns, leading to a decline in some populations of fish and coral reefs. However, advances in technology have also provided solutions to these issues, such as the development of sustainable fishing practices and technologies for cleaning up marine pollution.

Finally, in 1866, discussions about the marine life of the New Hebrides Archipelago were primarily confined to academic circles and satirical journals. By 2025, thanks to advancements in communication technology, these discussions have become more widespread and accessible to a broader audience. Social media platforms and online forums allow for the sharing of scientific findings and public engagement in conservation efforts, with many individuals taking an active role in protecting marine ecosystems.

⚗️ Papua
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Nautilus, captained by Captain Nemo, stopped at Papua, a region discovered by the Portuguese in 1511. The crew observed various marine species such as cetaceans, tuna, sea bream, sparidae, parrotfish, mussels, sea urchins, and corals. In contrast, in 2025, advanced submarine technology allows for detailed studies of these same marine ecosystems, with researchers using non-invasive methods to monitor the health and diversity of these species. For instance, satellite tagging technology is now used to track cetacean migration patterns, providing valuable insights into their behavior and habitat preferences.

In 1866, the Arfak Mountains were a sight to behold, but in 2025, the use of drones and remote sensing technologies allows for a more comprehensive understanding of these landscapes. For example, high-resolution satellite imagery can be used to map the terrain, monitor deforestation, and track changes in biodiversity over time. Additionally, the development of virtual reality technology enables researchers to explore these environments in greater detail without having to physically travel there.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, the Nautilus crew encountered the Papuans, leading to a battle that resulted in casualties on both sides. In 2025, interactions between humans and indigenous communities are guided by principles of respect and cooperation. For example, collaborative research projects are often established with local communities, ensuring that their knowledge and perspectives are integrated into scientific studies. This approach not only promotes cultural sensitivity but also helps to ensure the long-term sustainability of these ecosystems.

In 1866, Captain Nemo expressed a desire for a "coral tomb" as a peaceful grave. In 2025, advances in marine archaeology and conservation have led to a greater appreciation for the importance of preserving coral reefs and other marine ecosystems. For instance, efforts are underway to protect critical habitats from threats such as overfishing, climate change, and pollution. Additionally, innovative technologies like 3D printing are being used to create artificial reef structures that can help restore damaged ecosystems and support the growth of new coral colonies.

- Des espèces marines différentes ont été observées, notamment le Dugong (manatee australienne) et la Baleine à bosse pygmée, espèces menacées dont les populations sont en déclin.

- Les sous-marins modernes disposent de propulseurs électriques plus performants que ceux du Nautilus, permettant des vitesses encore plus élevées.

- La technologie de l'époque a permis de découvrir et d'étudier les récifs coralliens autour du cap Wessel, qui abritent une grande diversité d'espèces marines.

⚗️ Cape Wessel
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, at Cape Wessel, the Nautilus observed two marine species: Parrotfish and Mussels. Today, in 2025, researchers have identified over a dozen additional species in the same region, including the Coral Trout, Clownfish, and Sea Turtles.

In 1866, the Nautilus was propelled by a mechanical screw, a remarkable feat of engineering for its time. Today, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) equipped with advanced propulsion systems and sensors are common in marine research, enabling detailed studies of ecosystems like that at Cape Wessel.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, the Nautilus was powered by an unspecified energy source, likely coal or oil. In contrast, modern AUVs use electric power, often sourced from renewable technologies such as solar panels and lithium-ion batteries, reducing their environmental impact significantly.

In 1866, the Nautilus was equipped with a protective electric field to ward off predators. Today, scientists use non-lethal acoustic deterrents and biomimetic materials inspired by marine life to protect underwater equipment from damage or intrusion by marine organisms. For instance, some AUVs are coated with shark skin-inspired surfaces to reduce biofouling.

⚗️ Mer de Timor
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

1. En 1866, le Nautilus se rendait dans la mer de Timor et évitait les récifs naturels comme Money et Victoria grâce à des cartes marines précises. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les navires utilisent des systèmes de navigation électroniques tels que le GPS pour éviter ces mêmes récifs, car ils sont équipés de cartes marines numériques et d'une technologie de vision sous-marine haute résolution.

2. En 1866, les membres de l'équipage du Nautilus observaient des espèces marines telles que le cétacé, le poisson perroquet, la coquille Saint-Jacques et le corail. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les scientifiques étudient ces mêmes espèces mais également d'autres espèces menacées telles que le dugong (manatee du Pacifique) ou le requin tigre, grâce à des techniques de recherche et d'observation plus avancées.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

3. En 1866, les membres de l'équipage du Nautilus enterraient leurs compagnons dans un cimetière sous-marin, sans aucune possibilité de marqueur funéraire sur la surface. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les corps des marins perdus sont retrouvés et identifiés grâce à des technologies telles que l'ADN et l'identification par imagerie satellitaire, ce qui permet de leur rendre un hommage approprié.

4. En 1866, le capitaine Nemo avait connaissance de l'île de Timor gouvernée par des radjahs, mais il n'y avait pas de communication régulière entre cette île et le reste du monde. Aujourd'hui en 2025, les habitants de l'île de Timor ont accès à Internet grâce à des réseaux de télécommunications modernes, ce qui leur permet d'avoir une communication régulière avec le reste du monde et de bénéficier des progrès technologiques.

⚗️ Timor Sea
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Nautilus navigated through the Timor Sea, a region rich with marine life such as cetaceans, parrotfish, oysters, mussels, shells, gastropods, corals, and zoophytes. The crew buried one of their own in an underwater grave, a practice that would be unimaginable today due to the respect for human remains and marine conservation efforts.

In contrast, by 2025, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) are commonly used for scientific exploration in the Timor Sea. These robots can collect data about marine life without disturbing the ecosystem, providing insights into species like the Timor Sea humpback whale, a subspecies of the common humpback whale that was first identified in 2017.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, Captain Nemo and his crew relied on traditional navigation methods to navigate the Timor Sea, using landmarks like Money reefs and Victoria reefs as guides. By 2025, advanced GPS systems are used for navigation, allowing precise tracking of the Nautilus' journey and enabling researchers to study the impact of climate change on marine ecosystems in the region.

In 1866, the Timor Sea was relatively unexplored, with many species yet to be discovered or identified. Today, while much is known about the marine life in the area, ongoing research continues to uncover new species and ecosystems, such as the deep-sea coral communities that were first documented using remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) in 2018. The Timor Sea remains a vital hub for biodiversity, with its rich marine life attracting researchers from around the world.

⚗️ Île Keeling
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

1. En 1866, l'île Keeling était inconnue du grand public, mais fut visitée par Charles Darwin et le capitaine FitzRoy lors de leur voyage sur la Beagle. Aujourd'hui, en 2025, l'île est bien connue pour son écosystème corallien unique qui attire des milliers de touristes chaque année.

2. En 1866, les observations scientifiques étaient effectuées à la main avec des instruments tels que le télescope et l'aide-mémoire. Aujourd'hui, l'île est équipée de stations météorologiques automatiques et de caméras sous-marines permettant une surveillance continue de son écosystème marin.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

3. En 1866, les espèces observées autour de l'île Keeling comprenaient des marsouins, cétacés, huîtres, coquillages, gastéropodes, astérie, méduses et coraux. Aujourd'hui, grâce à la technologie d'identification automatique de poissons tels que le système SMART (System for Monitoring and Analysis of Real-Time), plus de 350 espèces différentes ont été identifiées autour de l'île.

4. En 1866, les connaissances scientifiques sur les coraux étaient encore limitées et leur rôle dans le maintien des écosystèmes marins était mal compris. Aujourd'hui, grâce aux avancées en génomique et en écologie marine, nous comprenons mieux comment les coraux contribuent à la biodiversité maritime et à lutter contre le changement climatique.

⚗️ Keeling Island
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, Keeling Island was a coral formation with magnificent coconut trees that had been previously visited by Charles Darwin and Captain FitzRoy. The marine life observed around the island included various fish species such as porpoises, cetaceans, oysters, shells, gastropods, starfish, jellyfish, and corals. The Nautilus, a fictional submarine from Jules Verne's novel "Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea," skimmed the shores of this desert island for a short distance.

In contrast, by 2025, Keeling Island has become a significant research station for climate change studies due to its location in the Indian Ocean, where the Mauna Loa Observatory measures atmospheric carbon dioxide levels. The marine life around the island remains diverse, but advanced technologies have enabled scientists to identify and study numerous new species, such as the discovery of the ghost shark (Chlamydoselachus anguineus) in 2017.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, no electric lights were available for underwater exploration, making it difficult to observe marine life at night. In contrast, by 2025, advanced LED lighting systems have been developed that allow researchers to study marine ecosystems and their behaviors more effectively, even in the deepest parts of the ocean. For example, these lights have helped scientists discover that some jellyfish are bioluminescent, emitting light through a chemical reaction within their bodies.

Lastly, in 1866, Keeling Island was visited by humans, but there were no automated underwater vehicles (AUVs) or remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) to explore the seafloor and collect data about marine life and geological features. In contrast, by 2025, AUVs and ROVs are routinely used for scientific research, allowing researchers to gather vast amounts of data on the ocean floor without putting human divers at risk. For example, an ROV named "Jason" was deployed in 2019 to study hydrothermal vents near Keeling Island, providing valuable insights into these unique ecosystems and their role in global carbon cycling.

⚗️ Golfe du Bengale
✨ Généré par Mistral (IA locale)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

En 1866, lors de l'escale du Nautilus au Golfe du Bengale, les observations des espèces marines étaient limitées à celles qui pouvaient être observées à la surface ou près de la surface des eaux. Les espèces observées comprenaient le marsouin, le requin, l'astérie, la méduse, le corail, l'alcyon, le polype et les zoophytes.

En 2025, grâce à des équipements de plongée sous-marine avancés et des robots sous-marins autonomes, des observations plus détaillées sont possibles dans les eaux profondes du Golfe du Bengale. Des espèces marines telles que le requin-tigre, la raie manta, le dauphin de rivière et le poisson-chien ont été observées dans des profondeurs supérieures à celles accessibles en 1866.

🌍 2025 — Aujourd'hui

En 1866, les connaissances sur les courants marins étaient limitées aux cinq principaux déterminés par la science, dont le Kuro-Scivo (ou Fleuve-Noir) dans l'Océan indien sud. En 2025, grâce à des satellites et des modèles numériques de haute résolution, les connaissances sur les courants marins ont considérablement progressé. De nombreux courants supplémentaires ont été identifiés, y compris des courants dans l'Océan indien nord, qui n'avaient pas été pris en compte en 1866.

En 1866, les techniques de cartographie maritime étaient limitées aux cartes manuscrites et à la triangulation terrestre pour déterminer les positions des lieux. En 2025, grâce à des satellites GPS et à des systèmes de positionnement par satellite précis, les cartes marines sont beaucoup plus précises et disponibles en temps réel. Les données sur la profondeur, la température, la salinité et les courants marins peuvent également être collectées automatiquement et transmises en temps réel aux stations météorologiques et aux centres de recherche.

⚗️ Bay of Bengal
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Bay of Bengal was a vast marine ecosystem teeming with life, home to various marine species such as porpoises, sharks, starfish, jellyfish, corals, soft corals, polyps, and zoophytes. The Nautilus, a fictional submarine from Jules Verne's novel "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea," encountered smooth-hound sharks with mosaic-like teeth and tiger sharks nearly six yards long. Tragically, the aftermath of the 1866 flood in India resulted in corpses being carried by the Ganges into the Bay of Bengal.

Fast forward to 2025, the Bay of Bengal remains a rich marine habitat, but the presence and impact of technology have significantly changed. Marine species are still present, including porpoises, sharks, starfish, jellyfish, and corals, but new discoveries have been made. For instance, the elusive Ganges river dolphin, once thought to be extinct, was rediscovered in the Bay of Bengal in 2011.

🌍 2025 — Today

In contrast to 1866, modern technology has allowed for more efficient and less destructive fishing methods. For example, the use of fish aggregating devices (FADs) has reduced bycatch and improved selectivity, helping to preserve marine biodiversity. However, overfishing remains a concern in the Bay of Bengal, with certain species like tiger sharks still under threat.

While in 1866, corpses floated on the surface due to natural disasters, in 2025, plastic waste from land-based sources has become a significant issue in the Bay of Bengal. Microplastics have been found in various marine species, posing a threat to their health and the food chain. The development of advanced technologies for plastic waste management and cleanup efforts is crucial for preserving the Bay of Bengal's marine ecosystem in the future.

In 2025, advanced submarine technologies would enable faster and more efficient exploration of the deep sea, including autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) that can operate for extended periods without human intervention. These advancements would greatly enhance our ability to study and protect marine ecosystems like the English Channel.

29.en:
Comparison 1866 / Today

⚗️ South of England Coast
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the _Nautilus_ stopped south of the English coast, a region teeming with diverse marine life such as common dolphins, harbor seals, and various species of fish like cod and bass. The crew observed these creatures from the Nautilus' windows, a novel technology at the time that provided a glimpse into the underwater world. In contrast to modern technology, their observations were limited by the capabilities of the submarine's electric lights and the physical limitations of the observation chambers.

Fast forward to 2025, the same region off the English coast is monitored by autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) equipped with advanced sonar systems and high-resolution cameras. These AUVs can map the seafloor in detail, providing insights into the distribution and abundance of marine species such as blue whales, which were rarely seen during Captain Nemo's time. Additionally, advances in genetic sequencing have allowed scientists to identify new species discovered in this region, like the deep-sea anglerfish (Melanocetus johnsonii), a creature that would have been unimaginable to Jules Verne and his readers.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, Captain Nemo's _Nautilus_ relied on sailing and electric propulsion to navigate the waters. Modern submarines, such as the British Astute-class, employ nuclear power for propulsion, allowing them to remain submerged for extended periods while maintaining high speeds. These submarines also use sophisticated sonar systems to detect and avoid obstacles, a significant improvement over the _Nautilus_' reliance on Captain Nemo's intuition.

In 2025, communication technology has advanced significantly compared to the telegraph used by Captain Nemo. Modern submarines are equipped with satellite communications, enabling them to maintain contact with surface ships and transmit data in real-time. This advancement allows for more efficient coordination between submarines and surface vessels during missions, a far cry from the isolation experienced by the crew of the _Nautilus_.

30.en:
Comparison 1866 / Today

⚗️ North of England Coast
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the North Sea teemed with a variety of marine life, including fish, seabirds, and occasional sightings of whales. The Nautilus, Jules Verne's fictional submarine, observed these species through its portholes, much like modern-day research vessels equipped with remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) do today. In contrast, the technology available in 1866 was limited; the Nautilus relied on hand-cranked propellers and simple observation equipment, while modern vessels use sonar systems, satellite navigation, and advanced imaging tools to study marine ecosystems.

In 1866, news of a rare species sighting could travel slowly due to the newly laid Atlantic cable, causing excitement among scientists and sailors alike. Today, in 2025, instant global communication via the internet allows for immediate sharing of scientific discoveries, such as the identification of new marine species or the tracking of migratory patterns. For example, the discovery of the elusive Greenland shark, a species thought to have been extinct until its rediscovery in 2016, would have reached a global audience within minutes in 2025.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, the Nautilus encountered stories and rumors about a mysterious flying creature that was said to have delayed telegrams in the Atlantic Ocean. Today, in 2025, such tales would be replaced by reports of underwater robots that have explored the deepest parts of the ocean, like the James Cameron-piloted DEEPSEA CHALLENGE submersible or the remotely operated vehicle (ROV) used during the National Geographic expedition to the Mariana Trench in 2012.

Lastly, in 1866, the discovery of a rare marine species could lead to an "arms race" among nations, as they raced to catch and study the creature. Today, in 2025, international cooperation is more common, with scientists sharing data and working together to protect and understand the world's oceans. For instance, the Census of Marine Life, a global initiative that ran from 2000 to 2010, aimed to assess the diversity, distribution, and abundance of marine life, providing valuable insights into the health of our planet's oceans.

Aujourd'hui, en 2025, ces espèces sont encore présentes dans cette région, mais des espèces invasives, telles que les anguilles royales asiatiques, ont également été observées, menaçant la biodiversité locale.

Aujourd'hui, en 2025, les navires sont équipés de systèmes de communication satellitaire permettant de communiquer en temps réel avec d'autres navires et les stations terrestres.

Aujourd'hui, en 2025, les navires doivent également faire face à des dangers marins tels que les ours polaires, les glaces flottantes et les vagues géantes, qui peuvent être causées par des phénomènes météorologiques extrêmes ou des activités humaines telles que l'exploration pétrolière.
31.en:
Comparison 1866 / Today

⚗️ Coast of Norway
✨ Generated by Mistral (local AI)
🕰️ 1866 — Jules Verne

In 1866, the Nautilus, Captain Nemo's submarine, navigated the treacherous coast of Norway, observing various marine species such as whales, sperm whales, narwhals, rays, seahorses, croakers, pearl oysters, and octopuses. The crew, consisting of Professor Aronnax, Conseil, and Ned Land, were unaware of the existence of DNA, a fundamental molecule that carries genetic information in every living organism, discovered by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953.

In contrast, in 2025, researchers exploring the same Norwegian coast would have access to advanced technologies like DNA sequencing machines, enabling them to study these marine species at a molecular level. For instance, they could analyze the genetic diversity of sperm whales, which are known for their large brains and complex social structures, or investigate the unique adaptations of narwhals, whose tusks may contain nanostructures that can be used in medical imaging technologies.

🌍 2025 — Today

In 1866, the crew's knowledge about marine ecosystems was limited compared to modern times. They were unaware of the concept of marine protected areas (MPAs), which are now crucial for preserving biodiversity and maintaining healthy ocean ecosystems. In Norway, there are numerous MPAs established to protect important habitats such as kelp forests and cold-water coral reefs.

In 2025, researchers would use satellite imagery and AI algorithms to monitor these MPAs effectively, ensuring their conservation efforts are targeted and efficient. For example, they could use remote sensing data to track the movements of whales within the protected areas, helping to minimize human-whale interactions and reduce the risk of entanglement in fishing gear. Additionally, they might employ autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) to explore deep-sea environments without disturbing fragile ecosystems, providing valuable insights into the biodiversity and ecology of these areas.
